summary: This lesson reviews the different uses of 'se' in Spanish (reflexive, phonetic double-pronoun change) and introduces a new use: 'se + me/te/le/nos/les' to express 'on me/on you/on him' etc., conveying that something happens to someone unintentionally or affecting them personally.

teachings.summary: Review of three uses of se: (1) reflexive se meaning 'self' (se queda = he stays/remains), (2) phonetic se replacing les before lo/la/los/las (se lo preparo = I prepare it for them), and (3) NEW: se + indirect object pronoun to express 'on me/you/him' — something happening to someone.
teachings.rule: Se + me/te/le/nos/les + verb = something happens 'on' someone (unintentionally/affecting them). Order: se + indirect object + verb.

examples.spanish: La batería siempre se me acaba
examples.english: The battery always runs out on me
Does the battery run out on you? (informal)
Does the battery run out on you? (formal)
The battery runs out on you guys/them

teachings.notes: Uses le (not lo) because it's 'on you' / 'to you'. This 'on me' structure is much more common in Spanish than English and not restricted to a few verbs.
